title: Question: Improving handling of gated clocks.
body:
---


Author Name: **Jeremy Bennett** (@jeremybennett)
Original Redmine Message: 1139 from https://www.veripool.org



---

Consider the following Verilog

```
    input clk;

    reg gated_clk_en = 1'b0 ;
    reg [1:0] pc = 2'b0;
    reg [1:0] res = 2'b0;

    wire gated_clk = gated_clk_en & clk;

    always @(posedge clk) begin
       pc <= pc + 1;
       gated_clk_en <= 1'b1;
    end

    always @(posedge gated_clk) begin
       res <= pc;
    end

```

Once the gated clock is enabled, one would expect the value of @res@ to lag that of @pc@ by one cycle. It is a delayed assignment, so in the second @always@ block, @res@ picks up the value of @pc@ _before_ it is incremented in the first block. A VCD trace shows this is what happens in simulation.

But in Verilator this is not the case. A VCD trace shows that @res@ tracks the value of @pc@ cycle by cycle. In the second @always@ block, @res@ picks up the value of @pc@ _after_ it is incremented in the first block. This would be correct if clk and gated_clk where independent. However in this case, one is derived from the other by continuous assignment, so the two @always@ blocks should be evaluated in the _same_ delta cycle, and _res_ should pick up the old value of _pc_.

The problem is caused in @V3Order.cpp@, which adds an extra loop in @processCircular()@ for internally generated clocks. The commenting indicates this is to fix races, as illustrated in @t_clk_dpulse@, which is an example of using MULTIDRIVEN signals.

In this example we can fix the result by marking @gated_clk_en@ using @/* verilator clock_enable */@. From looking at V3Order.cpp, I think this effectively breaks the loop, even if the generated clock vertex is marked circular.

However should this really be necessary? Finding all the clock enables in a big design is laborious. I tried disabling the code for in @processCircular()@ for internally generated clocks. This caused 4 regression failures. However two of those were tests which are marked to fail with Verilator, but pass in simulation, so arguably it is a neutral change (those two could now be marked as passes).

I'm not really advocating this, or at least only with a flag to control it. But I wonder if there is a more subtle test that could be used in @processCircular()@ to only add that loop when it is really needed. Any ideas?

What I'd really like is a way to indicate the relationship between the two clock domains in the above example, to get a proper ordering. They are separate domains, so I can't just merge the domains. V3Order.cpp will determine that @pc@ is in the @@(posedge clk)@ domain, with its PRE and POST in the @@(posedge clk or posedge v.gated_clk)@ domain. @res@ is in the @@(posedge v.gated_clk)@ domain, with its PRE and POST in the @[settle]@ domain. I can merge the @@(posedge clk)@ and @@(posedge clk or posedge v.gated_clk)@ domains, but that doesn't get me far enough. I can't merge in the @@(posedge v.gated_clk)@, since it is active less often than the @@(posedge clk)@ domain, let I need to show there is a relationship, so that PRE assigns in the two are done together, followed by general assignments, followed by POST assigns. Is there a way I can add a dependency between @res@ and the POST of @pc@, to keep the ordering correct.

So far my experiments with V3Order.cpp have demonstrated a) how hard it is to change and b) that I still don't really understand the code properly. I've a number of ideas on improvements in this area, but I thought I'd start the discussion here first.
